result:
The typewriter sits before me like an animal waiting to be fed. Its keys are teeth, each one poised to bite down on the page and leave a mark. I haven’t used a real typewriter in years, but in my mind, it’s always there—this antique machine with its sticky ‘e’ and the bell that rings at the end of every line, a small reminder that some things must come to an end whether we like it or not.

I think often about endings. About the way a sentence wraps itself around an idea and then, with a period, simply stops. There is a finality to it that both comforts and terrifies me. We spend our lives trying to avoid periods, reaching instead for semicolons and commas, anything to keep the thought going just a little bit longer. But the bell always rings. The carriage always returns.

The room around me is quiet except for the hum of the refrigerator and the distant tick of a clock I keep meaning to fix. It’s one of those old wind-up clocks with a yellowed face and roman numerals that have taken on the color of weak tea. It belonged to my grandfather, who died when I was twelve. I remember him winding it every night before bed, his thick fingers surprisingly delicate as they turned the small key. He would hold it up to his ear afterward, listening to its heartbeat, and nod once to himself as if confirming something important.

I do the same thing now, though the clock sits on a shelf and I rarely think to wind it. When it stops, I sometimes let days pass before I give it life again. I wonder if my grandfather would be disappointed. Probably not. He was a patient man who understood that neglect is just another form of attention, the way a shadow proves the existence of light.

Outside the window, a crow lands on the telephone wire. It tilts its head and regards me with one obsidian eye. I’ve always liked crows. There’s an intelligence in them that feels almost human, or perhaps we share something older than human—an ancient recognition between two creatures who know what it is to bury things and later return to them. I once read that crows remember faces and can hold grudges for years. I try to be kind to them, just in case. I leave them bits of bread and shiny objects on the windowsill: a bent spoon, a marble, the foil from a chocolate bar. They take the bread but never the trinkets, as if to say, *We don’t need your bribes. We already know who you are.*

The crow takes flight, and I watch it cut through the gray sky until it’s just a smear of ink against the clouds. Then I look back at the typewriter. The page is still blank. There’s a certain kind of pressure that comes with a blank page, a tyranny of possibility. Every word is a choice, and every choice closes off a thousand others. It’s easier, sometimes, to not choose at all. To let the white space remain white, a field of snow unmarked by footprints. But even snow eventually melts, and what’s left is just the same old ground underneath.

I think about my mother, who kept a journal her entire life but never let anyone read it. When she died, I found a stack of notebooks in her closet, their covers worn soft as fabric. I opened one at random and saw my own name. The entry was dated thirty years ago, when I was a child. She wrote about the way I laughed—a sudden, explosive sound that seemed to surprise even me. She wrote that she hoped I would never lose that laugh, that the world wouldn’t sand it away. I closed the notebook quickly, feeling like an intruder in my own life. I still haven’t read the rest. I’m not sure I ever will. Some stories are meant to remain unfinished, held in that trembling space between knowing and not knowing.

The refrigerator hums a little louder, then settles. The clock ticks. I place my fingers on the typewriter keys. They’re cool to the touch, almost cold. The ‘e’ key is indeed slightly sticky; it hesitates before rising back to position, as if considering whether it wants to participate at all. I press it anyway. *E*, the most common letter in the English language. Without it, everything falls apart. Words would stumble, sentences would gasp. It’s humbling to think that language relies so heavily on a single shape, a simple loop and line.

I type: *The crow knew something I didn’t.* The words appear on the page with a satisfying clack, each letter a small victory over silence. I stare at the sentence. It feels true, though I can’t explain why. Crows know things. They carry secrets in the gloss of their feathers, in the way they watch us from telephone wires and treetops. Maybe they see the world not as a series of objects but as a web of relationships, a living network of cause and effect that we’re too busy to notice. Maybe they know the exact moment the clock will stop, the precise day the bread will run out, the inevitable return of the man who sits at the typewriter and tries to make sense of the noise inside his head.

I keep typing. *It knew that I would sit here today, trying to fill a page with something that matters. It knew that I would fail.* I pause. The word “fail” sits on the page like a stone dropped into still water. Ripples spread outward: the failed ambitions, the failed relationships, the failed attempts to be a better person. We carry our failures with us, but they’re not always heavy. Some of them become so familiar they’re almost comforting, like a pebble you’ve rubbed smooth in your pocket for years. You reach for it without thinking, and there it is, cool and round and undeniably yours.

I think about a woman I loved once. She had a habit of biting her lower lip when she was thinking, and her laughter was like a door opening into a room full of light. We spent three summers together, and then one autumn she was gone. Not dramatically—there was no fight, no betrayal—just a gradual drifting apart, the way continents separate over millennia, so slowly you don’t notice until there’s an ocean between you. I still have a photograph of her somewhere, tucked into a book I no longer read. Her face in that photo is turned slightly away, as if she’s already looking toward a future that doesn’t include me. I wonder if the crow saw her leaving. I wonder if it tried to warn me and I simply didn’t understand.

The typewriter bell rings. I’ve reached the end of the line. My hand moves to push the carriage return lever, that satisfying mechanical sweep that resets everything and gives you a fresh line, a new beginning. It’s one of the most hopeful sounds I know. No matter what you’ve written—a confession, a complaint, a fragment of a memory—you can always start again. The page has room for all of it.

I type: *But failure is just a word, and words can be rewritten.* The ‘e’ sticks again, but I don’t mind. I’m learning to appreciate resistance. Without it, everything would be too easy, and nothing truly easy is ever worth doing. The crow returns to the wire outside. This time, it has something in its beak—a strand of tinsel, maybe, or a piece of string. It drops it onto the windowsill and flies away. An offering, or maybe a trade for all the gifts I’ve left. I smile. There’s a conversation happening here, one that transcends language, one that has been going on long before I sat down at this typewriter and will continue long after I stand up.

The clock chimes the hour, a soft, melodic tone that seems to come from another era. I realize I’ve been writing without knowing what I was writing, letting the words lead me instead of the other way around. Perhaps that’s the secret—to stop trying so hard to control the narrative and simply let it unfold. The page is no longer blank. It’s filled with letters and spaces, with small mistakes and corrections, with the inky fingerprints of a mind trying to make sense of itself. It’s not a masterpiece. It’s not even a complete thought. But it’s something. It’s proof that I was here, on this gray afternoon, with the crow and the clock and the ghost of my grandfather winding time in his hands.

I take the page out of the typewriter and hold it up to the light. The letters are uneven, some darker than others, the ‘e’ slightly raised where the key stuck. Imperfect. Alive. I fold it carefully and place it on the windowsill next to the tinsel the crow left. Let the world read it if it wants. Let the wind take it. Let the rain blur the ink until the words are just suggestions, just memories of themselves.

Outside, the sky is darkening. The crow is gone. The refrigerator hums. The clock ticks. And I, I sit here with ink on my fingers, already thinking about the next page, the next bell, the next beginning.

message.reasoning:
We are asked: "You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you." So I need to produce a 1000-word piece of writing, free-form. I'll just start writing stream-of-consciousness, or perhaps a narrative, or an essay. Since it's "whatever comes to you," I'll let my mind wander. I'll aim for exactly 1000 words. I'll write a story or a meditation. I'll keep count. 

Let me begin.

I'll write a story or a meditation. I'll keep count. 

Let me begin.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
